prompt: PCA was performed on the full 3K panel using two different SNP filtering strategies: (1) MAF>0.05 genome-wide, and (2) MAF>0.05 computed within each subpopulation separately (union of SNPs passing in any subpopulation). The PC1-PC2 plots differ in the relative spacing of subpopulation clusters. Interpret why.
context:
PCA comparison:

Strategy 1 (global MAF>0.05, 205,418 SNPs):
  PC1: 32.4% variance, separates indica from japonica
  PC2: 14.1% variance, separates tropical/temperate japonica and positions aus
  indica-japonica distance on PC1: 0.44 units
  aus-indica distance on PC1: 0.05 units

Strategy 2 (per-subpop MAF>0.05, union = 412,804 SNPs):
  PC1: 28.1% variance, separates indica from japonica
  PC2: 11.8% variance
  indica-japonica distance on PC1: 0.38 units
  aus-indica distance on PC1: 0.09 units

Key difference: Strategy 2 includes ~207K additional SNPs that are common in one subpopulation but rare or monomorphic in others.
correct_answer: The two strategies produce different PCA geometries because MAF filtering changes which variants contribute to the analysis: Strategy 1 (global MAF>0.05) retains only variants that are common across the full panel. This enriches for variants where indica and japonica have divergent allele frequencies (since together they dominate the panel), amplifying the indica-japonica axis on PC1 (0.44 units). However, variants private to smaller subpopulations (aus, aromatic) are more likely excluded, compressing their representation. Strategy 2 (per-subpop MAF>0.05 union) includes an additional 207K SNPs that are common within specific subpopulations but rare or absent globally. These include aus-private and aromatic-private variants that add information about within- and between-subpopulation variation not captured by Strategy 1. This has two effects: (a) The indica-japonica distance decreases (0.38 vs 0.44) because the additional SNPs dilute the strongly differentiated global-MAF variants. (b) The aus-indica distance increases (0.09 vs 0.05) because aus-private common variants now contribute to PCA, better resolving aus as a distinct cluster. The practical tradeoff: Strategy 1 emphasizes the dominant population structure axis (good for broad classification). Strategy 2 better resolves minor subpopulations (good for fine-scale structure analysis) but at the cost of noisier PC axes due to the inclusion of more subpopulation-specific variants. For multi-subpopulation studies, Strategy 2 or an intermediate approach (e.g., MAF>0.01 globally) is recommended to avoid under-representing small groups.
rubric: Full marks (3): Must explain how global vs per-subpop MAF filtering changes the SNP composition, connect to the geometry change (japonica distance decreases, aus distance increases), and discuss the tradeoff for structural analysis. Mostly correct (2): Identifies the filtering effect but missing one direction of the geometry change. Partial (1): Notes the plots differ without explaining the filtering mechanism. Incorrect (0): Claims the difference is due to sample size or PCA algorithm.
notes: MAF filtering strategy has a major but underappreciated effect on PCA geometry in structured populations.
